title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 9 (Fragment 1)
text:
The Magicians of Caprona

Chapter 9

“If you’re not careful,” said Angelica, glowering under the bulge of her forehead, “I shall sing the first spell that comes into my head. And I hope it turns you into a slug.”

That was a threat indeed. Tonino quailed a little. But the honor of the Montanas was at stake. “Take back what you said about my family,” he said.

“Only if you take back what you said about mine,” said Angelica. “Swear by the Angel of Caprona that none of those dreadful lies are true. Look. I’ve got the Angel here. Come and swear.” Her pink finger jabbed down at the tabletop. She reminded Tonino of his school teacher on a bad day.

He left his creaking chair and leaned over to see what she was pointing at. Angelica fussily dusted away a shower of yellow varnish to show him that she indeed had the Angel, scratched with the useless tap into the top of the table. It was quite a good drawing, considering that the tap was not a good gouge and had shown a tendency to slip about. But Tonino was not prepared to admire it. “You’ve forgotten the scroll,” he said.

Angelica jumped up, and her flimsy chair crashed over backwards. “That does it! You’ve asked for it!” She marched over to the empty space by the windows and took up a position of power. From there, with her hands raised, she looked at Tonino to see if he was going to relent. Tonino would have liked to relent. He did not want to be a slug. He sought about in his mind for some way of giving in which did not look like simple cowardice. But, as with everything, he was too slow. Angelica flounced around, so that her arms were no longer at quite the right angle.

“Right,” she said. “I shall make it a cancel-spell, to cancel you out.” And she began to sing.

Angelica’s voice was horrible, sharp and flat by turns, and wandering from key to key. Tonino would have liked to interrupt her, or at least distract her by making noises, but he did not quite dare. That might only make things worse. He waited while Angelica squawked out a couple of verses of a spell which seemed to center around the words turn the spell around , break the spell off . Since he was a boy and not a spell, Tonino rather hoped it would not do anything to him.

Angelica raised her arms higher for the third verse and changed key for the sixth time. “Turn the spell off, break the spell around—”

“That’s wrong,” said Tonino.

“Don’t you dare put me off!” snapped Angelica, and turned around to say it, which sent the angle of her arms more thoroughly wrong than ever. One hand was now pointing at a window. “I command the unbinding of that which was bound,” she sang, cross and shrill.

Tonino looked quickly down at himself, but he seemed to be still there, and the usual color. He told himself that he had known all along that such a bungled spell could not possibly work.

There came a great creaking from the ceiling, just above the windows. The whole room swayed. Then, to Tonino’s amazement, the entire front wall of the room, windows and all, split away from the side walls and the ceiling, and fell outwards with a soft clatter—a curiously soft sound for the whole side of a house. A draught of musty-smelling air blew in through the open space.

vocabulary.definition: A rounded swelling or protuberance that distorts a flat surface.
vocabulary.example: Angelica, glowering under the bulge of her forehead.

vocabulary.definition: Done clumsily or incompetently; botched.
vocabulary.example: Such a bungled spell could not possibly work.

vocabulary.definition: A magical spell intended to negate or nullify another spell.
vocabulary.example: She said, “I shall make it a cancel-spell, to cancel you out.”

vocabulary.definition: To have something as the main or most important subject or point.
vocabulary.example: The spell seemed to centre around the words turn the spell around.

vocabulary.definition: A continuous rattling sound as of hard objects falling or striking each other.
vocabulary.example: It fell outwards with a soft clatter.

vocabulary.definition: To give an authoritative order.
vocabulary.example: She said, 'I command the unbinding of that which was bound.'

vocabulary.definition: A harsh, high-pitched sound made by some bodies under strain or movement.
vocabulary.example: There came a great creaking from the ceiling.

vocabulary.definition: To divert attention away from something.
vocabulary.example: He considered distracting her by making noises.

vocabulary.definition: A current of cool air in a room or other confined space.
vocabulary.example: A draught of musty-smelling air blew in through the open space.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely bad or unpleasant.
vocabulary.example: None of those dreadful lies are true.

vocabulary.definition: Light and insubstantial; easily damaged.
vocabulary.example: Her flimsy chair crashed over backwards.

translations.ru: раздражённо двигаться, идти с важным видом

vocabulary.definition: To move with exaggerated jerks or movements, often to show annoyance.
vocabulary.example: Angelica flounced around.

vocabulary.definition: To look angry or sullen by narrowing the eyebrows.
vocabulary.example: Angelica, glowering under the bulge of her forehead, threatened Tonino.

vocabulary.definition: High respect or esteem; the quality of knowing and doing what is morally right.
vocabulary.example: But the honor of the Montanas was at stake.

vocabulary.definition: To stop the continuous progress of an activity or process.
vocabulary.example: Tonino would have liked to interrupt her.

vocabulary.definition: To poke suddenly or rapidly with a finger or pointed object.
vocabulary.example: Her pink finger jabbed down at the tabletop.

vocabulary.definition: Having a stale, moldy, or damp smell.
vocabulary.example: A draught of musty-smelling air blew in.

vocabulary.definition: To feel or show fear or apprehension.
vocabulary.example: Tonino quailed a little.

vocabulary.definition: To abandon or mitigate a harsh intention or cruel treatment.
vocabulary.example: Tonino would have liked to relent.

vocabulary.definition: Oppressively constant; persistent.
vocabulary.example: Angelica's voice was horrible, sharp and flat by turns.

vocabulary.definition: High-pitched and piercing in sound.
vocabulary.example: She sang, cross and shrill.

vocabulary.definition: To separate or break off from something.
vocabulary.example: The entire front wall of the room... split away from the side walls.

vocabulary.definition: To make a loud, harsh noise or cry.
vocabulary.example: Angelica squawked out a couple of verses of a spell.

vocabulary.definition: A risk or something to be won or lost in a situation.
vocabulary.example: The honor of the Montanas was at stake.

vocabulary.definition: To move slowly or rhythmically backward and forward or from side to side.
vocabulary.example: The whole room swayed.

vocabulary.definition: The act of releasing from a binding or constraint.
vocabulary.example: I command the unbinding of that which was bound.

vocabulary.definition: A stanza of a poem or song; a line or group of lines in poetry.
vocabulary.example: She sang a couple of verses of a spell.
